AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 1: Basic Economic Concepts (Topic 1.1-1.6)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Basic Economic Concepts for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 1: Basic Economic Concepts, Topic 1.1-1.6). Students should understand the foundational principles that underpin all economic analysis. Key concepts: scarcity (unlimited wants, limited resources) is the fundamental economic problem; opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative forgone; the production possibilities curve (PPC) illustrates trade-offs, efficiency, and economic growth; comparative advantage determines who should produce what (produce where opportunity cost is lowest); factors of production (land, labor, capital, entrepreneurship); economic systems (market, command, mixed) differ in how they answer the three fundamental questions (what, how, for whom to produce). Students should construct and interpret PPC graphs, calculate opportunity costs, determine comparative advantage from production data, and understand how specialization and trade benefit all parties. Vocabulary: scarcity, opportunity cost, PPC, trade-off, comparative advantage, absolute advantage, factors of production, specialization, marginal analysis.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 2: Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle (Topic 2.1-2.3)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Measuring Economic Performance for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 2: Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle, Topic 2.1-2.3). Students should understand how economists measure the overall health and output of an economy. Key concepts: Gross Domestic Product (GDP) measures the total market value of all final goods and services produced within a country in a year; the expenditure approach (GDP = C + I + G + Xn); the income approach; nominal GDP vs. real GDP (adjusted for inflation using a price index); GDP deflator and CPI (Consumer Price Index) measure the overall price level; real GDP per capita as a measure of living standards; limitations of GDP (excludes non-market production, underground economy, environmental degradation, and income distribution). Students should calculate GDP, convert between nominal and real GDP, compute inflation rates using price indices, and evaluate GDP as a measure of well-being. Vocabulary: GDP, nominal GDP, real GDP, GDP deflator, CPI, inflation, final goods, intermediate goods, expenditure approach, base year.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 3: National Income and Price Determination (Topic 3.1-3.7)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers National Income and Price Determination for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 3: National Income and Price Determination, Topic 3.1-3.7). Students should understand the aggregate demand and aggregate supply model that determines the price level and real GDP. Key concepts: aggregate demand (AD) shows the inverse relationship between the price level and real GDP demanded; AD shifts due to changes in consumer spending, investment, government spending, and net exports; short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) shows the positive relationship between the price level and real GDP supplied; SRAS shifts due to changes in input prices, productivity, and supply shocks; long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) is vertical at the full-employment level of output; macroeconomic equilibrium occurs where AD intersects SRAS; recessionary and inflationary gaps. Students should graph and shift AD, SRAS, and LRAS curves, identify equilibrium, and analyze how shocks affect output and prices. Vocabulary: aggregate demand, aggregate supply, SRAS, LRAS, macroeconomic equilibrium, recessionary gap, inflationary gap, price level, real GDP, potential output.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 3: National Income and Price Determination (Topic 3.2-3.5)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Aggregate Supply and Aggregate Demand for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 3: National Income and Price Determination, Topic 3.2-3.5). Students should understand how changes in aggregate demand and supply affect macroeconomic outcomes. Key concepts: demand-pull inflation occurs when AD increases, raising both the price level and real GDP in the short run; cost-push inflation occurs when SRAS decreases, raising the price level while reducing real GDP (stagflation); the multiplier effect amplifies changes in spending (spending multiplier = 1/(1-MPC) = 1/MPS); the tax multiplier is smaller than the spending multiplier (tax multiplier = -MPC/MPS); the balanced budget multiplier equals 1; the self-correcting mechanism: in the long run, wages and prices adjust to restore full employment. Students should use the AD-AS model to analyze the effects of demand and supply shocks, calculate multipliers, and understand both short-run and long-run adjustments. Vocabulary: demand-pull inflation, cost-push inflation, stagflation, multiplier effect, MPC, MPS, self-correction, wage adjustment, spending multiplier, tax multiplier.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 3: National Income and Price Determination (Topic 3.6-3.7)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Fiscal Policy for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 3: National Income and Price Determination, Topic 3.6-3.7). Students should understand how the government uses taxing and spending to influence macroeconomic outcomes. Key concepts: expansionary fiscal policy (increased government spending or decreased taxes) shifts AD right to combat recession; contractionary fiscal policy (decreased government spending or increased taxes) shifts AD left to combat inflation; automatic stabilizers (progressive income taxes, transfer payments like unemployment insurance) automatically counteract economic fluctuations without new legislation; the crowding-out effect (government borrowing raises interest rates, reducing private investment, partially offsetting fiscal stimulus); time lags (recognition lag, legislative lag, implementation lag) reduce fiscal policy effectiveness; supply-side economics argues tax cuts increase LRAS by incentivizing work and investment. Students should graph fiscal policy effects using AD-AS and explain crowding out. Vocabulary: fiscal policy, expansionary, contractionary, automatic stabilizers, crowding out, budget deficit, surplus, government spending, taxation, multiplier.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 4: Financial Sector (Topic 4.4-4.6)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Monetary Policy for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 4: Financial Sector, Topic 4.4-4.6). Students should understand how the Federal Reserve uses monetary policy tools to influence the money supply, interest rates, and economic activity. Key concepts: the Federal Reserve's tools include open market operations (buying/selling government bonds), the discount rate (interest rate on loans to commercial banks), and the reserve requirement (fraction of deposits banks must hold); expansionary monetary policy (buying bonds, lowering rates) increases the money supply, lowers interest rates, and stimulates investment and AD; contractionary monetary policy (selling bonds, raising rates) decreases the money supply, raises interest rates, and reduces AD; the federal funds rate is the target rate for overnight interbank lending; the money market model shows how money supply and demand determine the interest rate. Students should graph the money market, show how Fed actions shift supply, and connect to AD-AS outcomes. Vocabulary: monetary policy, Federal Reserve, open market operations, discount rate, reserve requirement, money supply, federal funds rate, expansionary, contractionary, interest rate.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies (Topic 5.2)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers the Phillips Curve for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies, Topic 5.2). Students should understand the relationship between inflation and unemployment. Key concepts: the short-run Phillips Curve (SRPC) shows an inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment (lower unemployment comes with higher inflation and vice versa); the long-run Phillips Curve (LRPC) is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment (NRU), indicating no long-run trade-off; the SRPC shifts when expected inflation changes or supply shocks occur; stagflation (high inflation with high unemployment) shifts the SRPC outward; when actual inflation exceeds expected inflation, unemployment falls below the NRU temporarily; in the long run, adaptive expectations adjust and the economy returns to the NRU. Students should graph both SRPC and LRPC, show how demand-side and supply-side changes affect both curves, and connect Phillips Curve analysis to AD-AS outcomes. Vocabulary: Phillips Curve, SRPC, LRPC, natural rate of unemployment, inflation expectations, stagflation, trade-off, demand-pull, cost-push, NAIRU.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies (Topic 5.6-5.7)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Economic Growth for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies, Topic 5.6-5.7). Students should understand the factors that drive long-run increases in an economy's potential output. Key concepts: economic growth is measured by increases in real GDP per capita over time; growth shifts the LRAS and PPC outward; sources of growth include increases in quantity/quality of labor (human capital through education and training), increases in physical capital (investment in machinery, infrastructure), technological progress (innovation, research and development), and improvements in institutions (property rights, rule of law, stable government); the production function relates inputs to output; diminishing returns to capital suggest that technology is the key driver of sustained growth; policies promoting growth include investment in education, R&D incentives, free trade, and infrastructure spending. Students should graph growth using PPC and LRAS shifts and evaluate growth policies. Vocabulary: economic growth, real GDP per capita, human capital, physical capital, technology, productivity, LRAS shift, PPC shift, diminishing returns, investment.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 6: Open Economy (Topic 6.1-6.2)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers International Trade for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 6: Open Economy, Topic 6.1-6.2). Students should understand the benefits of international trade and the arguments for and against trade barriers. Key concepts: countries benefit from trade by specializing in goods where they have a comparative advantage (lowest opportunity cost); trade barriers include tariffs (taxes on imports), quotas (quantity limits on imports), and subsidies; arguments for free trade (lower prices, greater variety, increased efficiency, economic growth); arguments for trade barriers (protecting infant industries, national security, preventing dumping, protecting jobs); the terms of trade (the price at which countries trade) must fall between the opportunity costs of the trading partners for both to benefit. Students should calculate comparative advantage from production data, determine terms of trade, and analyze the effects of tariffs and quotas using supply and demand graphs for imported goods. Vocabulary: comparative advantage, tariff, quota, free trade, trade barrier, terms of trade, export, import, specialization, dumping, subsidy.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 6: Open Economy (Topic 6.3-6.4)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Foreign Exchange Markets for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 6: Open Economy, Topic 6.3-6.4). Students should understand how exchange rates are determined and how they affect international trade and capital flows. Key concepts: an exchange rate is the price of one currency in terms of another; in a flexible (floating) exchange rate system, currency prices are determined by supply and demand in the foreign exchange market; demand for a currency comes from foreigners wanting to buy a country's goods, services, or financial assets; supply of a currency comes from domestic residents wanting to buy foreign goods, services, or assets; appreciation (currency becomes more valuable) makes imports cheaper and exports more expensive; depreciation (currency becomes less valuable) makes imports more expensive and exports cheaper; interest rate differentials, inflation differentials, and income growth affect exchange rates. Students should graph the foreign exchange market and analyze how changes in economic conditions affect exchange rates. Vocabulary: exchange rate, appreciation, depreciation, foreign exchange market, floating rate, fixed rate, demand for currency, supply of currency, capital flow.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 6: Open Economy (Topic 6.1)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers the Balance of Payments for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 6: Open Economy, Topic 6.1). Students should understand how a country records its international economic transactions. Key concepts: the balance of payments consists of the current account and the financial (capital) account; the current account records trade in goods and services, investment income, and transfers (a trade deficit means imports exceed exports); the financial account records purchases and sales of financial assets (stocks, bonds, real estate, foreign direct investment); the two accounts must balance (a current account deficit is offset by a financial account surplus and vice versa); a country with a trade deficit is a net borrower from abroad; a country with a trade surplus is a net lender. Students should understand how the accounts relate, why they must sum to zero, and how trade deficits and surpluses affect a country's international financial position. Vocabulary: balance of payments, current account, financial account, trade deficit, trade surplus, net exports, capital flows, foreign direct investment, transfer payments.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 2: Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle (Topic 2.4-2.5)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Inflation and Unemployment for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 2: Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle, Topic 2.4-2.5). Students should understand how these two key indicators are measured and their effects on the economy. Key concepts: unemployment rate = (unemployed/labor force) x 100; types of unemployment include frictional (between jobs), structural (skills mismatch), cyclical (due to economic downturns), and seasonal; the natural rate of unemployment (NRU) includes frictional and structural but not cyclical; full employment means zero cyclical unemployment, not zero total unemployment; inflation is a sustained increase in the general price level; CPI measures consumer prices; costs of inflation include decreased purchasing power, menu costs, shoe-leather costs, and arbitrary redistribution of wealth between borrowers and lenders; deflation can be more dangerous than moderate inflation. Students should calculate unemployment rates, identify types of unemployment, and analyze inflation's effects on different groups. Vocabulary: unemployment rate, labor force, frictional, structural, cyclical, natural rate, inflation, CPI, deflation, purchasing power, real vs. nominal.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 2: Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle (Topic 2.6)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers the Business Cycle for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 2: Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle, Topic 2.6). Students should understand the recurring pattern of expansion and contraction in economic activity. Key concepts: the business cycle consists of four phases: expansion (growing GDP, falling unemployment), peak (maximum output before downturn), contraction/recession (declining GDP, rising unemployment), and trough (minimum output before recovery); a recession is typically defined as two consecutive quarters of declining real GDP; leading economic indicators (stock market, building permits, consumer confidence) predict future economic activity; lagging indicators (unemployment rate, CPI) confirm trends; coincident indicators (GDP, industrial production) move with the economy. Students should identify business cycle phases, connect them to changes in unemployment, inflation, and output, and understand how fiscal and monetary policy attempt to smooth the business cycle. Vocabulary: business cycle, expansion, peak, contraction, recession, trough, recovery, leading indicator, lagging indicator, GDP growth, output gap.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies (Topic 5.6)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Long-Run Economic Growth for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies, Topic 5.6). Students should understand what determines an economy's long-run growth trajectory and potential output. Key concepts: long-run growth is driven by increases in productivity (output per worker) rather than short-run demand changes; the aggregate production function shows how inputs (labor, capital, technology) combine to produce output; technological progress shifts the production function upward, allowing more output from the same inputs; physical capital accumulation is subject to diminishing returns, so technology is the key to sustained growth; policies promoting long-run growth include investing in education and human capital, encouraging savings and investment, supporting research and development, maintaining stable institutions, and promoting free trade. Students should distinguish between short-run fluctuations and long-run growth trends and evaluate growth policies. Vocabulary: long-run growth, productivity, production function, technology, physical capital, human capital, diminishing returns, potential output, per capita income.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 4: Financial Sector (Topic 4.1-4.3)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Money and Banking for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 4: Financial Sector, Topic 4.1-4.3). Students should understand the functions of money, the banking system, and how money is created. Key concepts: money serves as a medium of exchange, unit of account, and store of value; commodity money (gold) vs. fiat money (government-decreed); the money supply measures include M1 (currency + demand deposits + traveler's checks) and M2 (M1 + savings, small time deposits, money market funds); fractional reserve banking means banks hold a fraction of deposits and lend the rest; the money multiplier (1/reserve requirement) shows how the banking system creates money through the lending process; the required reserve ratio determines how much banks must hold. Students should calculate the money multiplier and trace how an initial deposit creates a multiple expansion of the money supply through the banking system. Vocabulary: money, medium of exchange, fiat money, M1, M2, fractional reserve banking, money multiplier, required reserves, excess reserves, demand deposits, money creation.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 4: Financial Sector (Topic 4.1-4.2)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Financial Markets for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 4: Financial Sector, Topic 4.1-4.2). Students should understand the loanable funds market and how it determines the real interest rate. Key concepts: the loanable funds market brings together savers (supply of loanable funds) and borrowers (demand for loanable funds); the real interest rate is determined by the intersection of supply and demand for loanable funds; supply comes from private savings, government surplus, and foreign capital inflows; demand comes from businesses investing, consumers borrowing, and government borrowing; government budget deficits increase demand for loanable funds, raising real interest rates (crowding out private investment); an increase in savings shifts supply right, lowering interest rates; the Fisher equation relates nominal interest rate, real interest rate, and expected inflation (nominal = real + expected inflation). Students should graph the loanable funds market and analyze how economic changes affect real interest rates and investment. Vocabulary: loanable funds, real interest rate, nominal interest rate, savings, investment, crowding out, Fisher equation, capital market, budget deficit, supply of loanable funds.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies (Topic 5.4-5.5)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Government Deficits and Debt for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies, Topic 5.4-5.5). Students should understand the relationship between government spending, revenue, deficits, and the accumulating national debt. Key concepts: a budget deficit occurs when government spending exceeds tax revenue in a given year; the national debt is the cumulative total of all past deficits minus surpluses; deficit spending is financed by selling government bonds; crowding out occurs when government borrowing competes with private borrowing, raising interest rates and reducing private investment; the debt-to-GDP ratio is a more meaningful measure than absolute debt level; Ricardian equivalence suggests that rational consumers save more when government borrows (anticipating future tax increases), offsetting stimulus effects; arguments about debt sustainability involve future growth rates vs. interest rates. Students should graph the crowding-out effect in the loanable funds market and evaluate arguments about the significance of government debt. Vocabulary: deficit, surplus, national debt, government bonds, crowding out, debt-to-GDP ratio, Ricardian equivalence, interest payments, fiscal sustainability.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 6: Open Economy (Topic 6.5-6.6)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Open Economy Macroeconomics for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 6: Open Economy, Topic 6.5-6.6). Students should understand how international capital flows and exchange rates interact with domestic economic policy. Key concepts: in an open economy, the current account and financial account are linked (a trade deficit is financed by capital inflows); higher domestic interest rates attract foreign capital, increasing demand for the domestic currency and causing appreciation; appreciation makes exports more expensive and imports cheaper, worsening the trade balance; this creates a potential conflict: expansionary fiscal policy raises interest rates (crowding out), attracts foreign capital, causes appreciation, and reduces net exports, partially offsetting the stimulus. Students should trace the chain of effects from fiscal and monetary policy changes through interest rates, exchange rates, and net exports, and understand how international linkages complicate domestic policy. Vocabulary: open economy, capital flows, exchange rate, appreciation, depreciation, net exports, trade balance, interest rate differential, capital account, current account.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 2: Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle (Topic 2.1-2.2)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Economic Indicators for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 2: Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle, Topic 2.1-2.2). Students should understand the key statistics used to measure and evaluate economic performance. Key concepts: GDP (real and nominal) measures total output; the unemployment rate measures labor market health; the inflation rate (measured by CPI and GDP deflator) measures price stability; real GDP growth rate indicates economic expansion or contraction; GDP per capita approximates living standards; leading indicators (stock prices, building permits, consumer expectations) predict future trends; lagging indicators (average duration of unemployment, prime rate) confirm trends; coincident indicators (industrial production, personal income) track current conditions; limitations of these indicators (GDP excludes non-market activity, CPI may overstate inflation due to substitution bias, unemployment rate excludes discouraged workers). Students should interpret economic data, identify trends, and evaluate the health of an economy using multiple indicators. Vocabulary: economic indicator, GDP, unemployment rate, inflation rate, CPI, GDP deflator, leading indicator, lagging indicator, labor force participation rate, real GDP.

AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.ref: AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies (Topic 5.1-5.3)
AP®︎/College Macroeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Economic Policy Debates for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies, Topic 5.1-5.3). Students should understand the major debates about how best to manage the macroeconomy. Key concepts: the debate between Keynesian economics (government intervention through fiscal policy to stabilize the economy) and classical/monetarist economics (markets are self-correcting, government intervention often does more harm than good); active vs. passive policy (should government try to fine-tune the economy or follow fixed rules?); discretionary policy vs. rules-based policy; supply-side economics (tax cuts stimulate growth by increasing incentives to work, save, and invest); the debate over the effectiveness of fiscal vs. monetary policy; policy time lags (recognition, implementation, impact) reduce effectiveness; the role of expectations in policy effectiveness (rational expectations theory). Students should evaluate arguments from different economic schools of thought and apply them to real-world scenarios. Vocabulary: Keynesian, classical, monetarist, supply-side, discretionary policy, rules-based, time lag, expectations, self-correcting, stabilization policy.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 1: Basic Economic Concepts (Topic 1.1-1.6)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Basic Economic Principles for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 1: Basic Economic Concepts, Topic 1.1-1.6). Students should understand the foundational principles that underpin microeconomic analysis. Key concepts: scarcity forces choices, and every choice has an opportunity cost (the value of the next best alternative); rational decision-making involves marginal analysis (comparing marginal benefit to marginal cost); the production possibilities curve (PPC) illustrates trade-offs, efficiency (points on the curve), inefficiency (points inside), and impossibility (points outside); comparative advantage and specialization explain why trade is mutually beneficial; factors of production (land, labor, capital, entrepreneurship) are scarce resources. Students should construct and interpret PPC graphs, calculate opportunity costs from data tables, determine comparative advantage, and apply marginal analysis to decision-making. The concepts of trade-offs and marginal thinking are foundational for all subsequent microeconomic topics. Vocabulary: scarcity, opportunity cost, marginal analysis, PPC, efficiency, comparative advantage, specialization, trade-off, marginal benefit, marginal cost.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 2: Supply and Demand (Topic 2.1-2.6)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Supply and Demand for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 2: Supply and Demand, Topic 2.1-2.6). Students should understand how prices and quantities are determined in competitive markets. Key concepts: the law of demand (inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded); demand shifters (income, preferences, prices of related goods, expectations, number of buyers); the law of supply (positive relationship between price and quantity supplied); supply shifters (input prices, technology, expectations, number of sellers, government regulation); market equilibrium occurs where supply and demand intersect; surpluses (excess supply above equilibrium) drive prices down; shortages (excess demand below equilibrium) drive prices up; government interventions include price ceilings (below equilibrium, causing shortages) and price floors (above equilibrium, causing surpluses). Students should graph supply and demand, shift curves for various scenarios, and determine new equilibrium outcomes. Vocabulary: demand, supply, equilibrium, surplus, shortage, price ceiling, price floor, demand shifter, supply shifter, quantity demanded, quantity supplied.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 2: Supply and Demand (Topic 2.3-2.5)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Elasticity for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 2: Supply and Demand, Topic 2.3-2.5). Students should understand how responsive buyers and sellers are to price changes. Key concepts: price elasticity of demand (PED) measures the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in price; elastic demand (PED > 1) means quantity is highly responsive to price; inelastic demand (PED < 1) means quantity is less responsive; unit elastic (PED = 1); determinants of elasticity (availability of substitutes, necessity vs. luxury, proportion of income, time); the relationship between elasticity and total revenue (elastic: lower price increases revenue; inelastic: lower price decreases revenue); cross-price elasticity (positive for substitutes, negative for complements); income elasticity (positive for normal goods, negative for inferior goods); price elasticity of supply. Students should calculate elasticity using the midpoint method and analyze its implications for pricing and revenue. Vocabulary: elasticity, elastic, inelastic, unit elastic, total revenue test, cross-price elasticity, income elasticity, midpoint method, substitutes, complements.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 1: Basic Economic Concepts (Topic 1.5-1.6)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Consumer Choice and Utility for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 1: Basic Economic Concepts, Topic 1.5-1.6). Students should understand how consumers make decisions to maximize their satisfaction. Key concepts: utility is a measure of satisfaction or happiness derived from consuming a good; total utility increases with consumption but at a decreasing rate; marginal utility is the additional satisfaction from consuming one more unit; the law of diminishing marginal utility states that each additional unit provides less additional satisfaction; the utility-maximizing rule states that consumers maximize utility when the marginal utility per dollar spent is equal across all goods (MUa/Pa = MUb/Pb); this rule explains why demand curves slope downward (as price increases, MU per dollar decreases, so consumers buy less). Students should apply the utility-maximizing rule to determine optimal consumption bundles and understand how it connects to the demand curve. Vocabulary: utility, marginal utility, total utility, diminishing marginal utility, utility maximization, marginal utility per dollar, consumer choice, satisfaction, optimal.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 3: Production, Cost, and the Perfect Competition Model (Topic 3.1-3.3)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Production and Costs for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 3: Production, Cost, and the Perfect Competition Model, Topic 3.1-3.3). Students should understand how firms produce goods and the cost structures they face. Key concepts: the short run has at least one fixed input; the long run has all variable inputs; the production function relates inputs to outputs; marginal product (MP) is the additional output from one more unit of input; diminishing marginal returns occur when MP begins to decline; total cost = fixed cost + variable cost; average total cost (ATC) = TC/Q; average variable cost (AVC) = VC/Q; marginal cost (MC) = change in TC / change in Q; the MC curve intersects ATC and AVC at their minimum points; economies of scale (decreasing LRATC), diseconomies of scale (increasing LRATC), and constant returns to scale in the long run. Students should calculate and graph all cost curves and understand the relationships between them. Vocabulary: fixed cost, variable cost, total cost, marginal cost, average total cost, marginal product, diminishing returns, economies of scale, short run, long run.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 3: Production, Cost, and the Perfect Competition Model (Topic 3.4-3.7)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Perfect Competition for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 3: Production, Cost, and the Perfect Competition Model, Topic 3.4-3.7). Students should understand the model of a perfectly competitive market. Key concepts: characteristics include many small firms, identical products, perfect information, and free entry/exit; firms are price takers (cannot influence market price); the demand curve facing an individual firm is perfectly elastic (horizontal) at the market price; profit-maximizing output is where MR = MC (and MR = P in perfect competition); economic profit = (P - ATC) x Q; in the short run, firms produce if P >= AVC (shutdown point) even if experiencing losses; in the long run, free entry and exit drive economic profit to zero (P = ATC at the minimum); the long-run supply curve depends on whether the industry has constant, increasing, or decreasing costs. Students should graph firm-level and market-level diagrams side by side and determine profit/loss outcomes. Vocabulary: perfect competition, price taker, MR = MC, economic profit, normal profit, shutdown point, break-even, long-run equilibrium, free entry, allocative efficiency.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 4: Imperfect Competition (Topic 4.1-4.3)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Monopoly for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 4: Imperfect Competition, Topic 4.1-4.3). Students should understand how a single-seller market operates differently from perfect competition. Key concepts: a monopolist is the sole seller with no close substitutes; sources of monopoly power include barriers to entry (legal barriers, patents, control of resources, economies of scale/natural monopoly); the monopolist faces the market demand curve (downward-sloping); marginal revenue is less than price because the firm must lower price on all units to sell additional units; profit maximization at MR = MC; the monopolist charges a price above MC, creating a deadweight loss (allocative inefficiency); monopolists earn economic profit in both the short run and long run (barriers prevent entry); natural monopoly occurs when one firm can serve the entire market at lower cost than multiple firms; government regulation of monopoly (price regulation, antitrust). Students should graph monopoly and identify profit, deadweight loss, and consumer/producer surplus. Vocabulary: monopoly, barriers to entry, price maker, deadweight loss, natural monopoly, antitrust, price discrimination, economic profit, market power.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 4: Imperfect Competition (Topic 4.4-4.5)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Oligopoly for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 4: Imperfect Competition, Topic 4.4-4.5). Students should understand markets dominated by a few large firms whose decisions are interdependent. Key concepts: oligopoly features few dominant firms, significant barriers to entry, and strategic interdependence (each firm's decisions affect and are affected by rivals' decisions); products may be identical (steel) or differentiated (automobiles); game theory provides the framework for analyzing strategic behavior; the prisoners' dilemma illustrates why firms may not cooperate even when cooperation would benefit both; collusion (firms agreeing to restrict output and raise prices) is illegal but tempting; cartels (like OPEC) are formal collusion agreements; the kinked demand curve model explains price rigidity; firms compete through non-price competition (advertising, quality, innovation) rather than price wars. Students should analyze payoff matrices, identify dominant strategies and Nash equilibrium, and understand why cartels tend to break down. Vocabulary: oligopoly, interdependence, game theory, prisoners' dilemma, collusion, cartel, Nash equilibrium, dominant strategy, kinked demand, payoff matrix.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 4: Imperfect Competition (Topic 4.6)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Monopolistic Competition for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 4: Imperfect Competition, Topic 4.6). Students should understand the market structure that combines elements of monopoly and perfect competition. Key concepts: monopolistic competition features many firms, differentiated products (real or perceived differences through branding, quality, location), relatively easy entry and exit, and some degree of market power; because products are differentiated, each firm faces a downward-sloping demand curve (like monopoly) but with many substitutes (making demand relatively elastic); short-run profit maximization at MR = MC (can earn economic profit or loss); in the long run, entry and exit drive economic profit to zero (P = ATC), but P > MC (not allocatively efficient) and firms produce below minimum ATC (excess capacity). Students should graph short-run and long-run equilibrium and compare monopolistic competition to both perfect competition and monopoly. Vocabulary: monopolistic competition, product differentiation, excess capacity, branding, short-run profit, long-run zero profit, non-price competition, advertising, elastic demand.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Units 3-4: Market Structures Comparison
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Market Structures for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Units 3-4: Market Structures Comparison). Students should understand how to compare and contrast the four market structures. Key concepts: the four structures differ along key dimensions: number of firms (many to one), type of product (identical to unique), barriers to entry (none to insurmountable), price-making ability (none to complete), long-run economic profit (zero to positive), and efficiency (both allocative and productive). Perfect competition is the efficiency benchmark (P = MC, P = minimum ATC in long run); monopoly is the least efficient (P > MC, deadweight loss); monopolistic competition has long-run zero profit but excess capacity; oligopoly's outcomes depend on the degree of competition or collusion. Students should be able to identify real-world examples of each structure, compare their graphical representations side by side, and evaluate the welfare implications (consumer surplus, producer surplus, deadweight loss) of each. Vocabulary: market structure, perfect competition, monopoly, oligopoly, monopolistic competition, efficiency, deadweight loss, barriers to entry, market power, welfare.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 5: Factor Markets (Topic 5.1-5.4)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Factor Markets for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 5: Factor Markets, Topic 5.1-5.4). Students should understand how the markets for resources (inputs) work, particularly the labor market. Key concepts: the demand for factors of production is a derived demand (derived from the demand for the goods they produce); marginal revenue product (MRP = MR x MP) measures the additional revenue from hiring one more unit of a factor; the profit-maximizing hiring rule is MRP = factor price (MRP = wage for labor); the MRP curve is the firm's demand curve for labor; the labor supply curve shows the relationship between the wage and the quantity of labor supplied; market equilibrium wage is where labor supply equals labor demand; monopsony (single buyer of labor) pays lower wages and hires fewer workers than a competitive labor market. Students should graph factor markets, determine profit-maximizing employment levels, and analyze monopsony and minimum wage effects. Vocabulary: factor market, derived demand, marginal revenue product, MRP, wage, labor market, monopsony, human capital, marginal product, hiring rule.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government (Topic 6.1)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Market Failure for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government, Topic 6.1). Students should understand when and why free markets fail to achieve allocatively efficient outcomes. Key concepts: market failure occurs when the free market does not produce the socially optimal quantity of a good; sources of market failure include externalities (costs or benefits imposed on third parties), public goods (non-excludable, non-rivalrous goods that the market underproduces), market power (monopoly/oligopoly producing too little at too high a price), asymmetric information (one party has more information than another, leading to adverse selection and moral hazard), and income inequality. When market failure occurs, government intervention may improve outcomes through taxes, subsidies, regulations, or direct provision. Students should identify the type of market failure present in a given scenario and evaluate potential remedies. Vocabulary: market failure, externality, public good, market power, asymmetric information, adverse selection, moral hazard, efficiency, deadweight loss, government intervention.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government (Topic 6.2-6.3)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Externalities for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government, Topic 6.2-6.3). Students should understand how costs or benefits that affect third parties lead to market inefficiency. Key concepts: negative externalities (pollution, noise, congestion) impose costs on others not involved in the transaction; the market overproduces goods with negative externalities because the social cost exceeds the private cost; positive externalities (education, vaccinations, research) provide benefits to others beyond the buyer and seller; the market underproduces goods with positive externalities because the social benefit exceeds the private benefit. Solutions: for negative externalities, use per-unit taxes (Pigouvian taxes), regulations, cap-and-trade systems, or property rights assignment (Coase Theorem); for positive externalities, use subsidies, government provision, or mandates. Students should graph externalities showing the divergence between private and social costs/benefits and identify the socially optimal quantity. Vocabulary: externality, negative externality, positive externality, social cost, private cost, social benefit, Pigouvian tax, subsidy, Coase Theorem, deadweight loss, optimal quantity.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government (Topic 6.4)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Public Goods for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government, Topic 6.4). Students should understand why certain goods cannot be efficiently provided by private markets. Key concepts: public goods are non-excludable (cannot prevent non-payers from consuming) and non-rivalrous (one person's consumption does not reduce availability to others); examples include national defense, street lighting, clean air, and public parks; the free-rider problem means individuals can benefit without paying, so private firms cannot profitably provide public goods (they are underproduced or not produced at all by markets); government provision funded by taxation is the typical solution; common resources are rivalrous but non-excludable (fishing grounds, clean water), leading to the tragedy of the commons (overuse); private goods are both excludable and rivalrous. Students should classify goods into the four categories (public, private, common resources, club goods) and explain why market failure occurs for each type. Vocabulary: public good, non-excludable, non-rivalrous, free rider, tragedy of the commons, common resource, club good, private good, government provision, market failure.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government (Topic 6.5)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Government Intervention for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government, Topic 6.5). Students should understand the tools governments use to correct market failures and their trade-offs. Key concepts: price controls (price ceilings cause shortages, price floors cause surpluses); per-unit taxes reduce quantity, create deadweight loss, and the tax burden is shared based on elasticity (more inelastic side bears more burden); per-unit subsidies increase quantity and can correct positive externalities; antitrust laws prevent monopolistic behavior; regulations set standards for safety, environment, and quality; government provision of public goods; redistribution through taxes and transfer payments. Students should analyze the effects of government intervention using supply and demand graphs, identify unintended consequences (black markets from price ceilings, surplus from price floors), evaluate whether the intervention achieves its goal, and calculate tax incidence based on relative elasticities of supply and demand. Vocabulary: price control, tax incidence, per-unit tax, subsidy, regulation, antitrust, deadweight loss, tax burden, elasticity, unintended consequence.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 5: Factor Markets (Topic 5.1-5.3)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Labor Markets for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 5: Factor Markets, Topic 5.1-5.3). Students should understand how wages and employment levels are determined in different labor market structures. Key concepts: in a perfectly competitive labor market, firms are wage takers and hire until MRP = wage; the market wage is determined by the intersection of labor supply and labor demand; factors affecting labor demand include productivity, price of the output, and technology; factors affecting labor supply include population, immigration, education, and alternative opportunities; wage differentials arise from human capital differences, compensating differentials (higher pay for unpleasant or dangerous work), discrimination, and union power; minimum wage above the equilibrium creates unemployment but raises income for employed workers; monopsony (single employer) pays below the competitive wage and hires fewer workers. Students should graph competitive and monopsony labor markets and analyze policy effects. Vocabulary: labor market, wage rate, MRP, labor demand, labor supply, human capital, compensating differential, minimum wage, monopsony, wage differential, union.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government (Topic 6.6)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Income Distribution for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government, Topic 6.6). Students should understand how income is distributed in market economies and government policies that address inequality. Key concepts: the Lorenz curve graphically represents income distribution (the further from the 45-degree line of equality, the greater the inequality); the Gini coefficient measures inequality (0 = perfect equality, 1 = perfect inequality); sources of income inequality include differences in human capital, inheritance, discrimination, market power, and globalization; poverty measures (poverty line, poverty rate); government redistribution tools include progressive taxation (higher earners pay higher rates), transfer payments (welfare, Social Security, Medicaid, food stamps), and in-kind benefits; the equity-efficiency trade-off (redistribution may reduce incentives to work and invest). Students should interpret Lorenz curves, discuss the trade-off between equity and efficiency, and evaluate redistribution policies. Vocabulary: income distribution, Lorenz curve, Gini coefficient, inequality, poverty, progressive taxation, transfer payments, redistribution, equity, efficiency trade-off.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 4: Imperfect Competition (Topic 4.5)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Game Theory for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 4: Imperfect Competition, Topic 4.5). Students should understand how strategic decision-making is modeled using game theory. Key concepts: game theory analyzes situations where the outcome depends on the choices of multiple decision-makers (players); a payoff matrix shows the outcomes for each combination of strategies; a dominant strategy is the best choice regardless of what the other player does; Nash equilibrium occurs when neither player can improve their outcome by unilaterally changing strategy; the prisoners' dilemma demonstrates why rational self-interest can lead to collectively suboptimal outcomes; in the classic prisoners' dilemma, both players defect even though mutual cooperation would be better for both; repeated games can sustain cooperation through strategies like tit-for-tat. Students should read and analyze payoff matrices, identify dominant strategies and Nash equilibria, and apply game theory to real-world oligopoly decisions (pricing, advertising, output). Vocabulary: game theory, payoff matrix, dominant strategy, Nash equilibrium, prisoners' dilemma, cooperation, defection, strategic behavior, tit-for-tat, interdependence.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 1: Basic Economic Concepts (Topic 1.6)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers Behavioral Economics for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 1: Basic Economic Concepts, Topic 1.6). Students should understand how real human decision-making deviates from the rational actor model assumed in traditional economics. Key concepts: traditional economics assumes rational, self-interested decision-makers with perfect information; behavioral economics (drawing on psychology) shows that people systematically deviate from rationality; bounded rationality means people make satisfactory rather than optimal decisions due to cognitive limitations; cognitive biases include loss aversion (losses feel worse than equivalent gains feel good), anchoring (over-relying on the first piece of information), status quo bias (preference for the current state), and present bias (overvaluing immediate rewards vs. future benefits); nudges are subtle policy interventions that guide behavior without restricting choice (opt-out vs. opt-in retirement savings). Students should identify how biases affect economic decisions and evaluate whether nudges can improve outcomes. Vocabulary: behavioral economics, bounded rationality, cognitive bias, loss aversion, anchoring, nudge, default option, present bias, heuristic, rational actor.

AP®︎/College Microeconomics.ref: AP Micro Unit 2: Supply and Demand (Topic 2.8-2.9)
AP®︎/College Microeconomics.prompt: This topic covers International Trade and Microeconomics for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 2: Supply and Demand, Topic 2.8-2.9). Students should understand how international trade affects domestic markets, prices, and welfare. Key concepts: trade is based on comparative advantage (producing where opportunity cost is lowest); when a country opens to trade at the world price, if the world price is below the domestic price, the country imports (consumers benefit, domestic producers lose); if the world price is above the domestic price, the country exports (domestic producers benefit, consumers pay more); tariffs raise the domestic price of imports, benefiting domestic producers and the government but harming consumers and creating deadweight loss; quotas restrict the quantity of imports, raising domestic prices similarly to tariffs but without government revenue; free trade maximizes total surplus but creates winners and losers. Students should graph the effects of trade, tariffs, and quotas on consumer surplus, producer surplus, government revenue, and deadweight loss. Vocabulary: comparative advantage, world price, domestic price, imports, exports, tariff, quota, consumer surplus, producer surplus, deadweight loss, free trade, protectionism.
